content: AF management involves two strategies: rate control vs rhythm control. Rate control targets resting HR <80 bpm (symptomatic) or <110 bpm (lenient strategy—RACE II trial showed noninferior outcomes). Agents: beta-blockers (first-line), non-DHP calcium channel blockers (diltiazem, verapamil; avoid in HFrEF), digoxin (adjunct, especially in HF). Rhythm control aims to restore/maintain sinus rhythm. EAST-AFNET 4 trial (2020) showed early rhythm control reduced CV outcomes in recently diagnosed AF. Cardioversion (electrical or pharmacological with flecainide, propafenone, ibutilide, amiodarone) for symptomatic patients. Antiarrhythmic drugs for maintenance: flecainide/propafenone (no structural heart disease), sotalol, dofetilide (requires monitored initiation), dronedarone (avoid in permanent AF or HFrEF), amiodarone (most effective but significant long-term toxicity). Catheter ablation (pulmonary vein isolation) superior to antiarrhythmics for maintaining sinus rhythm (CABANA, FIRE AND ICE trials); first-line option for symptomatic paroxysmal AF, especially young patients. Surgical Maze procedure during cardiac surgery. All patients still need anticoagulation based on CHA2DS2-VASc regardless of rhythm strategy.
